result.markdown:
--- title: "Plotting my next move" jupyter: brainvomit execute: echo: false warning: false --- In the Summer of 2024, my lease was up. Instead of looking for an apartment, I procrastinated by making a map of NYC's most desirable neighborhoods, which is universally agreed to consist of three things: 1. Subways 2. Trees 3. Food ## 1 Subways If you know me, you know that I am lazy. I am willing to walk 11 mins *max* from my apartment to the subway. That said, if the walk happens to pass by multiple coffee shops and the station can get me downtown in 20 mins, I may be willing to go a little farther... clearly, even my basest instincts recognize there is some nuance to what it means to be 'well-connected'. A subway station can be valuable in two ways: 1) It can get you to a desirable place, and 2) It *is* the desirable place. Number 1 hints at some graph shenanigans going on here. If you can capture the NYC subway system as a graph — each node a station, each edge the line(s) connecting them — then you can calculate the eigenvector centrality of each station, which is exactly what I did below. ::: {#cab8fdb2 .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](nyc_files/figure-html/cell-3-output-1.png){width=443 height=704} ::: ::: Intuitively, the eigenvector centrality answers: is this station connected to other stations that are themselves well-connected? It is not enough for one station to be connected to many others — the others must themselves be well-connected. To measure whether the station *itself* is desirable, I use ridership. The more people swipe in, the more desirable it is. The final centrality score is an equal- weighted average of ridership and centrality.* *For the nitpicky reader, I'd rather have computed a node-weighted eigenvector centrality, but this gets me 90% of the way there out-of-the-box, and that's good enough for me.* ::: {#5d9bf918 .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
Station Name Centrality Score
334 Times Sq-42 St 1.000
68 42 St-Port Authority Bus Terminal 0.959
61 34 St-Penn Station 0.767
228 Grand Central-42 St 0.729
59 34 St-Herald Sq 0.591
109 86 St 0.586
14 14 St-Union Sq 0.571
46 23 St 0.565
13 14 St 0.523
170 Canal St 0.490
223 Fulton St 0.463
97 72 St 0.455
85 59 St-Columbus Circle 0.401
113 96 St 0.396
9 125 St 0.369
84 59 St 0.349
122 Atlantic Av-Barclays Ctr 0.312
246 Jackson Hts-Roosevelt Av 0.310
103 8 Av 0.308
98 74 St-Broadway 0.307
``` ::: ::: ::: {#f1fabffa .cell execution_count=4} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: *The centrality score is on a 0–1 scale, but because the data is highly skewed towards Manhattan, the color range maxes out at the 95th percentile.* Clearly, NYC proper is very well-connected. But there are pockets in the outer boroughs that are *relatively* well-connected — even more relevant if you want to stay away from the hustle while still getting around quickly. ## 2 Trees Kind of insane that there's a decadal census of each sidewalk tree in NYC (individual health, diameter, species). It excludes park trees, so this is less about proximity to green spaces and more the overall green-ness of walking around. ::: {#3cb723be .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: *Tree counts have been normalized to a 0–1 score.* ## 3 Restaurants I grabbed all restaurants that had an inspection between 2023–2024, plus new restaurants not yet inspected. ::: {#01398320 .cell execution_count=6} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Overall score Since the number of trees happened to distinguish residential areas well, I weigh it twice as much as the other two to break up the Manhattan monolith. ::: {#5f39f8da .cell execution_count=7} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Summary Honestly, I don't know how useful this was. It mostly confirmed my intuitions about the best neighborhoods (LIC, Williamsburg, Dumbo, Forest Hills, Bushwick). You'll also notice it inflates Manhattan, since the city proper ranks high on all three. Maybe later I'll add a penalty for commerciality (Midtown is great for work and shopping... but who wants to live there?) or affordability. ## Appendix 1. [NYC 2015 tree census](https://data.cityofnewyork.us/Environment/2015-Street-Tree-Census-Tree-Data/uvpi-gqnh/about_data) 2. [NYC 2023 ridership](https://data.ny.gov/Transportation/MTA-Subway-Origin-Destination-Ridership-Estimate-2/jsu2-fbtj) 3. [NYC subway stations shapefile](https://data.ny.gov/Transportation/MTA-Subway-Stations/39hk-dx4f/about_data) 4. [NYC subway lines shapefile](https://geo.nyu.edu/catalog/nyu-2451-34758) 5. [NYC borough boundaries](https://data.cityofnewyork.us/City-Government/Borough-Boundaries/tqmj-j8zm) 6. [NYC restaurant inspections](https://data.cityofnewyork.us/Health/DOHMH-New-York-City-Restaurant-Inspection-Results/43nn-pn8j/about_data)
